nativeRange.habitat: Found along reef slopes and drop-offs, typically at depth, in tropical Indo-Pacific and Central Pacific waters.

diet.notes: Feeds primarily on zooplankton in the wild; in aquaria has been observed accepting frozen mysis shrimp, enriched frozen brine shrimp, and copepods. Multiple small feedings per day are generally recommended to keep this species in good condition.

summary: A slender, magenta-bodied planktivore from Indo-Pacific reefs, generally considered reef-safe and best kept in groups of five or more with open swimming space and multiple daily feedings.
careNotes:
The Slender Whitleyi Anthias tends to be a delicate species that generally does best in well-established reef systems rather than newly set-up tanks. Groups of five or more are broadly recommended, as the shoaling structure appears to provide a greater sense of security, reducing stress and encouraging regular feeding. Like many anthias, this species is an active, constant grazer (meaning it feeds in frequent, small bursts throughout the day rather than in one or two large meals), so multiple small feedings each day are generally advised to keep individuals in good condition. A refugium — a small connected compartment where copepods, amphipods, and other tiny crustaceans grow naturally — has been reported to usefully supplement the diet between targeted feedings.

The Slender Whitleyi Anthias is widely considered a high escape risk, and a tightly fitting lid or mesh cover is strongly recommended for any tank housing this species. Slow acclimation to new water conditions is generally advised, and introducing the group before adding more boisterous or territorial tankmates tends to improve outcomes.

Males and females of this species share very similar coloration, making sex determination more difficult than in many other anthias, which often show marked color differences between the sexes. Like all anthias, Luzonichthys whitleyi is a protogynous hermaphrodite — meaning individuals are born female, and the dominant female in a group will transition to male if the group's male is lost. Adult size reported across sources spans a range of roughly 4 to 7.6 cm, with variation likely reflecting both measurement methodology and regional differences. Care level assessments in the hobby range from moderate to advanced; the combination of feeding pickiness, group requirement, and escape risk places this species toward the more demanding end of the spectrum.

additional.name: FishBase
additional.notes: Cross-checked adult size (max 5.8 cm), temperature range (modeled 27.2–29.1°C), native range, family (Anthiadidae), and order. FishBase temperature range is higher than hobby sources; averaged for final value.

additional.name: SaltwaterFish.com
additional.notes: Cross-checked size (3–4 in), temperature (74–79°F / 23–26°C), pH (8.1–8.4), salinity (1.023–1.025), tank size (55 gal), grouping behavior (4+), and lifespan (3–5 years).

additional.name: Reeflex
additional.notes: Cross-checked adult size (5.8 cm / 2.28 in), temperature (23–27°C), minimum tank size (750 L for advanced care), care level (advanced only), diet (mysis, brine shrimp, zooplankton). Tank size of 750 L vs. 55-gal retail recommendation is a >2× spread; biased toward larger due to schooling requirement and active swimming.
